messages.content: hi
Hello! How can I assist you today?

"Resume

Glossary

Table of contents
General information	1
Preface	2
Resume	3
1.0	Introduction	6
2.0. Context & Orientation	7
2.1. Company description	7
2.2 Business orientation	8


Introduction

Automation, a phenomenon that has brought major changes to our society and economy. The exponential growth in technologies such as artificial intelligence, machine learning, robotics and data analytics has led to the evolutions that companies and industries are currently undergoing.

	Also the software development company “companyX” strives for the next level in automation. One of the ways this can be achieved is in inventory management. A concrete example concerns the provision of complete lunches and sufficient drinking facilities during the day. The current inventory management process is based on manual registration, which regularly results in unwanted shortages of drinks or excessive purchases of a certain product. companyX therefore aims to prevent this problem by automating the relevant process.

The aim of this product accountability report is to provide insight into and document the development process. The report will go into detail on the aforementioned issue and provide an overview of all the processes, methods and practices used to develop the final solution to this problem. In addition, this report also takes a closer look at the technologies used, the architecture that has been set up and the actual realization of this product.
	  
2.0. Context & Orientation

2.1. Company description
“Our strength lies in developing smart but simple solutions for complex problems,” emphasizes Rein Buseman, a companyX participant. Although companyX falls within the Information and Communication category, they take a unique approach.





The place where software professionals come together to work on products and projects that both they and their clients can be proud of. They value the power of ambition and free will. They support the idea that the human touch should be reflected in everything they do, so that professionals can deliver high-quality work.

The professionals at companyX have been using their expertise for countless different organizations for 20 years. They have worked on projects ranging from solutions for automation in healthcare to contactless payments at festivals and smart charging stations for electric cars. Their work covers almost every conceivable area of ​​work and emphasize that their work is always tailor-made, with a number of important similarities.

Moreover, companyX attaches great importance to involving the right “participants” who are a perfect fit for realizing the requested dream. These are people who get energy from solving a certain issue.



companyX also uses an approach without management layers. In this way they ensure that the stakeholders of every project, regardless of its size, are closely involved in the process. This approach promotes clear and direct communication, resulting in the desired end result.
2.2 Business orientation

2.3 Project management & system landscape

2.3.1 Process
companyX uses the agile approach. This method of project management is aimed at creating a flexible and efficient process. They attach great importance to correctly prioritizing tasks and testing partial products with users as quickly as possible.
	
	Moreover, they apply the scrum methodology.	 companyX does this to promote Agile working, they report on their website (BRON). The most important thing is that they work in short iterations, also called sprints. With every sprint there is the opportunity to respond to new situations or insights, so the process remains flexible.

As mentioned earlier, companyX also uses an approach where there are no management layers. This means that the stakeholders and product owner talk directly to the developers. This ensures clear and direct communication, which only improves the end result.

2.3.2 “The colors”
CompanyX currently consists of different teams. These teams are called colors; each team has its own color and catchy name. Such a team consists of a maximum of 12 participants, the cell division principle is used by creating multiple teams. Each color also has its own category. Some projects fall under that category and will often be carried out in that color.

	CompanyX does plan to abandon this method. More information?
















2.3.3 Systems & Tools
Of course, companyX also uses various systems and landscapes. An Azure environment is preferred for most projects. Although the Microsoft landscape is most common within the company, more attention is now being paid to whether more integration with the AWS landscape can take place. The aim of this is to promote the diversity of the software platforms, so that they are not completely tied to Azure. However, it is important to remember that the choice depends entirely on the project and the participants. Does it fit the use case well? Is there enough expertise in that area, or is there space and material to acquire enough knowledge? In addition, companyX also uses their self-made systems. If they think that certain software can be improved or they simply want a system tailor-made for them, they build it themselves. AIventory, the product of this accountability, is an example of this.

CompanyX also uses various tools that optimize the work process. Examples of this include:


Slack: A business messaging application that connects people with the information they need. Slack works with so-called channels, where you can share information with your colleagues. In addition to these channels, it is also possible to message a person directly, both within and outside your organization. See:https://slack.com/ 

Gather: A digital working environment in which you can walk up to colleagues with a personal character, sit together at a conference table and actually see and hear them. Since companyX is a work from home company. See:https://www.gather.town/ 



Base60:An application built by companyX that serves to manage most of the paperwork. This contains all information regarding costs/turnover, all registered participants, purchasing of materials, etc.
3.0. problem analysis

3.1 Problem context
companyX is a meeting place where professionals come together to make dreams come true. In order to maintain this “sanctuary” it is important to take care of both the physical and mental health of all participants. Furthermore, this company has a strong commitment to sustainability, but in addition to green energy, food waste is of course also an important concern.

Lunch is available at all times for all participants and there is a refrigerator where everyone can take drinks from. If these supplies run out, they can be replenished with products from a separate stock room. This space should, if all goes well, be equipped with several crates or trays of soft drinks, sandwich fillings, any snacks and an extra refrigerator. Maintaining this stock is currently entrusted to a volunteer. This is done manually because there is currently no other way to monitor stock. Without someone continuously monitoring this, this will result in empty shelves.

3.2 Problem definition

Due to the voluntary and manual stock management of facilities such as drinks or sandwich fillings, companyX participants are often left with a shortage or surplus of products. This increases the risk that participants do not drink enough, which means there is a risk that they will work less productively or with discomfort. And when a surplus of products has been ordered, this can also lead to food waste. Which is contrary to the business goals set by companyX. At this time, there is no alternative method for tracking inventory status.


' give me general feedback

It seems like you have the beginnings of a comprehensive report, with a clearly structured table of contents and defined sections. Here's some feedback based on the content provided:

1. Table of Contents:
   - The table of contents appears to be clear with a logical structure. However, ensure that each section and subsection is correctly numbered and aligned with its respective page number. For example, the "Resume" appears twice – as a main section and as a subsection under "Preface". You might want to rectify that to avoid confusion.

2. Introduction:
   - The introduction outlines the context and relevance of automation in the industry, and it does a good job setting the stage for companyX's initiative on inventory management automation.

3. Company description (2.1):
   - There is a good description of companyX's ethos, focusing on their strengths and unique approach. The quote from a company participant adds a personal touch and helps to explain their philosophy.

4. Context & Orientation (2.0):
   - This section appears to be well thought out, explaining the guiding principles and approaches of companyX. For instance, their preference for the agile approach and scrum methodology is indicative of adaptability and customer focus.

5. Project management & system landscape (2.3):
   - This section does well to highlight companyX's project management style and the operational tools they use. The details about team organization and their use of color-coded teams is interesting, though it is mentioned that this method might be abandoned without further detail. Readers might find it helpful if this is expanded upon.

6. Problem Analysis (3.0):
   - The problem context and definition are clearly presented. It's good that the company's commitment to sustainability and productivity is highlighted as part of the problem context. It's clearly stated why the current manual inventory management system is not sufficient.

General Suggestions:
- Ensure consistency in numbering and formatting throughout the document. Each section should be clearly distinguishable.
- Consider including information about the consequences of not solving the inventory management problem to strengthen the case for why automation is necessary.
- Proofread the document for typos or grammatical errors (for example, "business orientation" should probably have a section number).
- Further elaboration in some sections might be beneficial, for instance, when mentioning companyX’s plans to change their team structure.
- Provide a clear explanation of how the report will unfold after the Problem Analysis section, possibly offering a glimpse into potential solutions or methodologies to be discussed.
- Lastly, it would be helpful to clarify the references. For example, "BRON" is mentioned but not explained. Ensure that all sources are properly cited.

Overall, the document is informative, well-organized, and outlines a clear problem that needs to be addressed. With a bit more detail in certain areas and some minor adjustments, the report will be an excellent resource for understanding companyX's approach to inventory management automation.

problem analysis

3.1 Problem context
companyX is a meeting place where professionals come together to make dreams come true. In order to maintain this “sanctuary” it is important to take care of both the physical and mental health of all participants. Furthermore, this company has a strong commitment to sustainability, but in addition to green energy, food waste is of course also an important concern.

Lunch is available at all times for all participants and there is a refrigerator where everyone can take drinks from. If these supplies run out, they can be replenished with products from a separate stock room. This space should, if all goes well, be equipped with several crates or trays of soft drinks, sandwich fillings, any snacks and an extra refrigerator. Maintaining this stock is currently entrusted to a volunteer. This is done manually because there is currently no other way to monitor stock. Without someone continuously monitoring this, this will result in empty shelves.

3.2 Problem definition

Due to the voluntary and manual stock management of facilities such as drinks or sandwich fillings, companyX participants are often left with a shortage or surplus of products. This increases the risk that participants do not drink enough, which means there is a risk that they will work less productively or with discomfort. And when a surplus of products has been ordered, this can also lead to food waste. Which is contrary to the business goals set by companyX. At this time, there is no alternative method for tracking inventory status.














3.3 Stakeholders 
3.3.1 Identification
It is important to know who exactly the stakeholders are in a project and what their goal or task is. To tackle this thoroughly, it is essential that we make an inventory of all possible parties involved in advance, classify them and analyze their role or importance.

Users:
The users provide input by stating their needs and requirements for the project. They can also contribute ideas to the development, after all, they are the people who will use the end product. They are also important to evaluate the results so that adjustments can be made if necessary.


Figure 1.0.0.0.AA Caption




















Design team:
The design team works on a design taking into account the requirements and wishes that have been drawn up. These stakeholders also manage the project, budget and resources.


Figure 2.0.0.0.AA
3.3.2 Matrix

how do you involve stakeholders, how do you obtain your requirements? (Stakeholder involvement and systematic) (Stakeholder communication plan?)

In the section on problem analysis, you've sketched a clear picture of the issues at hand regarding inventory management at companyX. Moving on to the identification of stakeholders and their involvement, you are developing a crucial aspect of the project framework. Here is some guidance on how to further structure this part of your report:

3.3 Stakeholders
3.3.1 Identification
Your identification of stakeholders is on track. It is key to recognize users as the primary stakeholders, as their satisfaction determines the success of the project. Including the design team highlights the operational side of stakeholder management. Consider also identifying other potential stakeholders such as suppliers, IT support, finance department, and senior management, as they all may have a vested interest in the project's outcome.

For each stakeholder, outline their specific roles, expectations, and potential impact on the project. A stakeholder analysis should delve into the interests of each party, the potential influence they exert, and strategies for managing their expectations and engagement throughout the project.

3.3.2 Matrix
To address how you plan to involve stakeholders and obtain requirements systematically, consider creating a stakeholder matrix or a RACI chart (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed). This chart helps to clarify the role of each stakeholder in different areas of the project and is particularly useful for ensuring clear communication and responsibility allocation.

Also, describe your approach to requirements gathering. This might involve interviews, surveys, workshops, or user testing sessions with stakeholders.

Stakeholder Communication Plan
A stakeholder communication plan should be outlined as follows:

**1. Objectives:**
   Define what the communication plan intends to achieve. This could be ensuring stakeholder engagement, updating on progress, managing expectations, or gathering feedback.

**2. Methods:**
   Identify the communication methods that will be used for engaging with different stakeholders. These might include regular meetings, email updates, dedicated Slack channels, project dashboards, etc.

**3. Frequency:**
   Decide how often each stakeholder group needs to be updated. Users might require weekly updates, whereas finance may only need monthly reports.

**4. Content:**
   Tailor the information provided to the interests and needs of each stakeholder group. For example, users will be more interested in feature updates, while senior management might care about return on investment and strategic alignment.

**5. Feedback Mechanisms:**
   Establish clear processes for stakeholders to give feedback. This might involve surveys, a suggestion box, or a designated contact person.

**6. Documentation:**
   Ensure that all communications are well-documented, and meeting minutes are distributed to keep everyone in the loop.

Figures and Captions
As for the figures mentioned (1.0.0.0.AA & 2.0.0.0.AA), ensure that they are directly related to the text and provide valuable insights. Perhaps they illustrate typical user requirements or the design process. Each figure should have a caption succinctly describing what the figure depicts and its relevance to the report.

Objective 
The goal is to develop an effective alternative for inventory management. The intention is to reduce the voluntary and manual aspect; the alternative must be possible for all participants to use and manual maintenance of a list must become a thing of the past.


3.5 Potential solutions
3.5.1 Intro
In short, the problem means that inventory management is currently mainly based on voluntary and manual registration. To solve this, the goal has been set to develop an alternative that tackles these two aspects. Various options have been considered and tested for this.

3.5.2 Approach	
First of all, the problem and the goal were carefully examined. What exactly is the purpose of the assignment and what do we want to achieve? The actual hard requirements had not yet been drawn up, which made it difficult to go into detail. In order to be able to come up with a targeted solution, “rough” requirements had been set for the time being; In any case, the alternative had to be an application, which in some way tackles the task of manually registering products. The application had to be able to know whether a product is present, which product exactly it is and register it.
	
	Subsequently, it was investigated which technologies could steer the project towards the right solution. Exactly which tech stack would be used was not yet interesting at this stage. It was mainly about which technology(ies) could best address the aforementioned "rough requirements" but at the same time was flexible enough to implement any extensions or adjustments based on the later actual requirements and wishes of the solution. .









3.5.3 Solution directions
In order to automatically recognize and register products, Deep-Learning options were quickly looked at. This quickly became a lot more complicated and it was also unknown territory. Moreover, this also requires you to have some idea of ​​the stock, which also added some complexity to the solution. There are also various options within this domain:

3.5.3.1 Image Classification
To begin with, “Image Classification” was examined. The idea was that an image was sent from inventory to the back-end application. This image is then processed and “chopped” into smaller parts. These smaller parts are then classified, giving you the name of the product that is in the smaller part of the image as output. However, this is not the ideal solution; there are too many situations where this can go wrong. It is difficult to divide an image into smaller parts like this without having two different products in the same part.


Figuur 3.0.0.0AA Image Classification vs Object Detection

3.5.3.2 Object Detection
Secondly, “Object Detection” was examined further. It was similar to Image Classification but without the problem of having to chop the image into parts first and then running the risk of having two different products in the same part. The difference can be clearly seen in the image above. It can recognize and label objects in an image. As output you will then receive all the objects found and where they are located on the image. It sounds like the ideal solution to our problem. The only disadvantage we could think of at that moment was that it is quite an arduous and complicated process.


3.5.3.2 State Comparison
“State Comparison” was also seen as a plausible idea. In this method you use a “Desired State” and a “Current State”, then you compare the two. It basically means comparing an image from a full stock with the image that was currently created using a vector database such as Weaviate. It was also proposed to establish fixed locations in the stock so that it could be concluded based on the location of the products whether or not it is present in the stock. But this was also not the ideal choice; a participant from companyX stated that there is never a time when the stock is completely full. And in addition, if the “Desired State” ever changes, it is a lot of hassle to adjust it in the application.

The objective and problem analysis leads into a discussion of possible solutions, all of which are technology-driven and designed to automate the inventory management process. Your approach indicates an understanding of the complexity of the challenge and a keen sense of exploration of available technologies to address it.

Here are some feedback and suggestions for improvement for each potential solution and the overall section:

### Objective Statement
Your objective statement is clear and defines the target well. It sets the stage for solutions that are user-friendly and eliminate manual tracking. It's good that you emphasize the need for the solution to be universally usable for all participants. However, it might be strengthened by also mentioning expected outcomes such as efficiency, cost savings, or error reduction.

### Potential Solutions (3.5)
#### 3.5.1 Intro
You concisely summarize the problem and your goal. This is good; however, add a sentence to reflect on why automation is beneficial, which may include accuracy, time-saving benefits or integration with existing systems.

#### 3.5.2 Approach
The approach section is somewhat vague, and it might serve you better to define clear, albeit preliminary, requirements before discussing the technologies. It could help focus the direction of the solution from the outset and avoid scope creep.

#### 3.5.3 Solution Directions
You've provided a solid initial review of various technological options, highlighting the importance of innovation in solving the problem. You also acknowledge the need for technologies that are both solution-oriented and flexible.

#### 3.5.3.1 Image Classification
You've correctly identified the limitations of using image classification in isolation, particularly concerning images with multiple overlapping products. This shows critical thinking and understanding of technical constraints. Instead of dismissing the idea entirely, you could suggest possible enhancements or auxiliary technologies that could overcome these limitations, like separating items before the image is taken.

#### 3.5.3.2 Object Detection
Object detection seems to be a promising direction; you've identified both its benefits and challenges. More details could be added about how you plan to approach the 'arduous and complicated' aspect of it. Speak to the feasibility of implementation and potential ways the process can be streamlined or simplified.

**Note on Section Numbering:** You seem to have two subsections labeled "3.5.3.2." It will be clearer if the second one is renumbered to "3.5.3.3" to distinguish it from the Object Detection section.

#### 3.5.3.3 State Comparison
The concept of "State Comparison" is innovative, but as you allude to, it has specific practical limitations. You could explore how technology might address these issues, such as developing algorithms that can learn and adapt the 'desired state' dynamically rather than a manual update.

**General Feedback:**
- Consider adding a summary at the end of the Potential Solutions section, outlining the pros and cons of each option to help guide the decision-making process.
- You might also include initial thoughts on the implementation complexity, cost, and potential impact of each solution to provide a more holistic view.
- Each solution would benefit from real-world examples or case studies where possible, citing how similar technological approaches have been successful or lessons learned from their application in other contexts.
- Ensure that the discussion on solutions thoroughly examines not just the capabilities of the technology, but also integrates user experience, maintenance, and scalability considerations.
- Keep the focus on how each solution aligns with the company values of sustainability and efficiency.
- For the figures mentioned (like Figuur 3.0.0.0AA), make sure they are accompanied by clear and informative captions that relate directly to the text and add value to the reader’s understanding.

Lastly, enhancing the interactivity and user-friendliness of the document could involve clickable links in digital versions or clear cross-references in printed formats to navigate between related sections and figures.

Approach

4.1 Intro
Before the start of the project, the best approach is investigated and a plan is drawn up for this. This plan mainly answers the "how questions" such as: "How will the project be divided?", "How will the communication proceed?", "How will the requirements be determined?" or “How do we deal with the risks?”. The answers to these questions provide guidelines to answer the issues but do not go into detail about the actual implementation. This chapter starts with an overview of the project structure and its different phases. The methodologies used that address specific issues are then described. It also explains how the requirements are determined and how communication with the stakeholders will proceed. In addition, it is explained in detail how the quality of the product is guaranteed, including a risk analysis that identifies all potential risks, their impact and the associated measures. Finally, a plan is drawn up that incorporates all the previously mentioned components into a clear and realistic schedule. 

4.1 Project phasing
Every larger project must be divided into smaller phases. This way the project retains a clear structure and it is easier to create smaller, more realistic sub-tasks. This project is also divided into several phases and milestones; both at a global level and purely during the implementation phase. If we look from a global perspective, you can divide the entire project into the following phases:

4.1.1 Research phase
During this phase, the assignment is looked at broadly and it is determined what the approximate goal is and what resources will be needed to best tackle this goal. Research is being conducted for this, but no specific requirements or wishes have yet been stated.

4.1.2 Analysefase 
This happens during the analysis phase. In this part of the project, the stakeholders are identified and the requirements and wishes are formulated in User Stories. Furthermore, the problem and the goal are worked out in more detail so that it is completely clear what exactly the intention is.

4.1.3 Designfase
Subsequently, in the design phase, designs are sketched, flows are visualized and the first prototypes or designs are presented to the stakeholders with the aim of determining the desired direction for implementation.

4.1.4 Implementation phase
Followed by the phase that lasts the longest. During this time, the detailed design is realized. The end result of this phase can then be divided into different milestones or further stages. The following stated stages are global; a detailed schedule can be found in the last paragraph of this chapter.

In the first stage the focus is on building a simple, working application. This application simply maintains a (mocked) status of a refrigerator or pantry. A shopping list is drawn up based on the information obtained from this. This includes intermediate results such as a fully operational web API, a working front-end and a set up database.



In the next stage the complexity starts to increase a bit. Deep Learning is introduced for the first time. A photo from the stock or refrigerator determines whether a product is present or whether it should be added to the shopping list. This photo must be passed through the recognition software and return a json format response. This also involves training your own model, or preparing the images through stitching, for example1 before the images are “scanned”.

                 In the third stage, the existing software is combined with hardware of your choice. The intention is to expand the application to be able to download photos via an external device such as a telephone. These photos are then automatically prepared and forwarded to the recognition software. Who finally calculates the shopping list.

               In the final stage specific hardware is used. There is a camera or other sensor that can take a snapshot both on demand and via an adjustable time loop and can send it via a wireless and automatic connection to the application that then carries out the steps to get the desired result.


Figure 4002AA Deployment Sketch, Stage 4

4.1.5 Finishing phase
Finally, the phase where code is optimized, software production is prepared and the finishing touches are put into place. To be clear, this is not the phase where the tests are written. These are written during the implementation phase after adding functionality.



4.2 Quality Assurance 

4.2.1 Intro
Ensuring high quality in a solution is related to using a structured approach and continuous but also thorough checks. To ensure that the solution meets the requirements and wishes of the client (and the stakeholders), there are various criteria and preconditions that Steve jobs must meet. This goes hand in hand with the ISO standard 25010, which describes the quality characteristics of software (ISO 25000, n.d.)

4.2.2 Functional Suitability
A logical but often underestimated factor is the constant tailoring of the chosen solution to the requirements and wishes of the client. To ensure that development remains in line with the stakeholders' initial ideas, regular communication and feedback sessions are very important. These ideas can also change over time and it is therefore important to have the opportunity to update them. This process is crucial for improving product quality, especially in the area of ​​Functional Suitability according to the ISO 25010 guidelines. It ensures a better alignment of the solution with the intended objectives.

 	In addition, another important step is to define acceptance criteria for each “User Story”. It was already mentioned that a structured approach is indispensable if you want to develop a high-quality product. That is why user stories were drawn up during the analysis phase based on the requirements and wishes of the stakeholders. These are then added to a “board” where they can easily be scheduled. These user stories can only be approved when they meet all pre-set acceptance criteria. These acceptance criteria are discussed in further detail in a later chapter.


4.2.3 Software Quality
To ensure technical quality, various testing methods have been used and a solid architecture that guarantees scalability, maintainability and security. This architecture is described and substantiated in detail in a later chapter. Communication also plays an important role here. The project was continuously monitored by technical experts and in case of complex issues there was plenty of expertise nearby to consult. During development, constant attention was paid to the quality characteristics described in the ISO term 25010 such as: Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability & Portability.
The tests are implemented as part of the development cycle, but not as a separate phase after the implementation phase. It is integrated into the implementation phase; the tests are written immediately after a new feature is added. This way you don't have to write all the tests at once at the end. In addition, one of the acceptance criteria of the user stories is that all tests pass for this functionality. Then it goes without saying that the results of the tests must be able to be concluded immediately. This way it is not necessary to wait until the implementation phase is completed to check off these user stories.

The unit tests focus on individual parts of the code to ensure that they function correctly and meet expectations. Moreover, these are executed automatically via Continuous Integration Continuous Delivery (CI/CD) pipelines. This means that an automated testing process is created with every change in the codebase.



Figure 5.0.0.2AA Flow of a User Story
	
In addition, end-to-end (E2E) testing is performed using a tool called Cypress. These tests simulate user interactions in the application and, for example, check whether performing a certain action, such as pressing a button, produces the desired result. This way of testing is very close to the actual production environment.

Finally, an indispensable factor that promotes software quality is the choice of a stable and flexible architecture based on different design patterns. It is important to pay attention to the associated best practices of this architecture and design patterns. This not only concerns matters such as project structures and names that match the chosen architecture, but also aspects that belong to the chosen technologies, such as memory management or the optimal use of assets. Many of the technologies chosen were personally unknown and therefore required extensive research. This research was mainly about finding out the best practices and the “Do's & Don'ts” of these technologies.

The approach section of your document outlines a comprehensive, multi-stage process for developing the inventory management system. You are discussing the importance of project phasing, quality assurance, and stakeholder engagement, which are all key components of a successful project. Here's some feedback to ensure clarity and thoroughness in your report:

### 4.1 Intro
- The introduction to 'Approach' gives a good overview of what you will cover in this chapter. It's important to specify that you will provide a high-level view and not detailed implementation plans, as this sets appropriate expectations for the reader.

### 4.1 Project Phasing
#### 4.1.1 Research phase
- You outlined the goal of the research phase effectively, stressing the focus on understanding the big picture and resource requirements.

#### 4.1.2 Analysis phase
- In the analysis phase, you emphasized the importance of stakeholder identification and formulating requirements, which is key to a project's success. Make sure to use consistent terminology through the document (e.g., 'Analysefase' should match the language of the report).

#### 4.1.3 Design phase
- The design phase as described appears to be iterative and collaborative, which are vital principles for Agile methods. Clarify the type of prototypes or designs (low-fidelity, high-fidelity, etc.) and their role in stakeholder feedback.

#### 4.1.4 Implementation phase
- The implementation phase is well-structured with clearly defined stages. Details about each stage give insight into the project's complexity and how it will unfold over time. Consider mentioning how you will address and integrate feedback during this phase.

**Include timelines for each phase or state that they will be detailed later to give the reader an idea of the project's duration.**

#### 4.1.5 Finishing phase
- It's important to stress that optimization and preparation for production are not afterthoughts but critical stages of the project. Clarifying when testing begins is great to avoid misconceptions about it being left to the end.

### 4.2 Quality Assurance
#### 4.2.1 Intro
- The introduction sets the tone for the rigorous and structured methods that will be applied to ensure the high quality of the product, which is reassuring for stakeholders.

#### 4.2.2 Functional Suitability
- You demonstrate an understanding of the importance of aligning development with stakeholder needs. Tying this section back to the ISO 25010 standard adds credibility. Explain briefly how regular communication and feedback sessions will be structured to ensure alignment with stakeholder needs.

#### 4.2.3 Software Quality
- The emphasis on testing methods and solid architecture is pivotal. You covered the bases of technical quality well. Mentioning consultation with experts is reassuring, emphasizing the value placed on domain knowledge. 

**Consider moving the detailed discussion of specific technologies such as CI/CD pipelines and Cypress closer to the implementation phase description.**

### Figures and Captions
- Figures and illustrations can enrich the report if they are relevant, clear, and well-integrated into the text. Make sure the figures you reference (e.g., Figure 4002AA and Figure 5.0.0.2AA) are close to their corresponding text sections and have captions that explain their content and relevance to the reader.

**General Feedback:**
- Make sure to maintain consistent language and terminology throughout the document.
- Incorporate a summary at the end of each major section to reiterate the key points and next steps.
- Stay cognizant of passive voice ("it is explained") vs. active voice ("we explain") in descriptions to maintain a clear and direct narrative style.
- Watch out for placeholder names ("Steve Jobs") that appear in the document and replace them with the appropriate names or titles as necessary.
- Ensure that references to figures, appendixes, or external references are accurate and can easily be cross-referenced by the reader.
- Emphasize flexibility and adaptability in your approach, as this is a sign of a mature and resilient project management strategy.

4.2.4 User Experience
In addition to the functional and software aspects, the user experience (UX) is of great importance for the quality of the end product (Ridder, n.d.). A positive user experience results in user satisfaction and therefore encourages more frequent use of the app. Additionally, an intuitive user interface and smooth workflows enable users to complete their tasks faster and work more efficiently. The ultimate goal of the application is to accelerate and improve the inventory management process through automation, so efficiency plays an important role.

To ensure that the quality of the user experience is guaranteed, there are a few essential steps that must be taken. It starts with analyzing the user needs and the flow of the application. After this analysis phase, it is important to create a user-friendly design that meets the needs of the users and at the same time the application flow.

  	In addition, there is a planned UX workshop/course on the program, organized by the company where the internship is taking place. While there is already existing knowledge and skills in the field of user experience, this workshop provides the opportunity to not only gain new insights, but also to connect with various UX experts. This network can be valuable for obtaining additional help and feedback to ensure the product meets the quality standards set by the experts.

 	During the development of the product, we work in an environment with other developers, often in a group consisting of fellow interns. These colleagues play an active role in extensively testing the product on various devices and platforms. Their feedback is the first “human check” that goes through a functionality, for example. In addition, several feedback sessions will also be planned in collaboration with various stakeholders. Stakeholders are given the opportunity to fully test the application, which provides valuable insights for optimizing the user experience.

	It is also a personal goal to add the Wow Factor to the design of the application. While the main goal remains a simplistic yet efficient design, it is not unimportant to give the product a unique and memorable look.


The goal is to create an iterative process where there is continuous development, testing and improvement. With the aim of continuing to work consistently and achieving the desired results.

In this analysis, the likelihood of risks is estimated and the extent of the consequences is examined. Finally, measures and actions are determined so that a conclusion and recommendations can be drawn.

Now that it is clear which risks have priority based on the consequences and likelihood of the risk, the measures can be determined. 4.4 Methodology

4.4.1 Intro
Methodical action provides useful guidelines and a solid basis that can be used when confronted with complex issues. How do you respond to situations for which you simply do not have the knowledge? How do you obtain the requirements? How do you act when you have a knowledge question? How are the project phases maintained? This subchapter describes the action plan for the situations just discussed. The realizations of these plans will be elaborated in a later chapter and linked back to this chapter.

4.4.2 Requirements
About 50% of all IT projects fail (Soest, 2021). That's quite an extreme ratio. While the cause is often quite easy to recognize; requirements of a project or system that do not actually solve the problem. These requirements are the basis of your system and without a solid foundation, you will not achieve the desired end result. In short, properly recording these points is extremely important and that is why a lot of attention is paid to this part of the development in this project.

It is important to have a clear idea of ​​where the requirements are derived from. The stakeholders are central to this process. The problem analysis determined which stakeholders there are and how much influence and importance they have. Furthermore, it is important to draw up a communication plan that accurately indicates when communication will take place with the various stakeholders, including how often and the method of communication. This document can be found in Chapter 4.X.X.X.

 	When these matters are clear, the requirements and wishes can be recorded according to the methods tailored to the relevant stakeholders as described in the aforementioned communication plan. These requirements and wishes are then formulated in the form of a user story. These stories must be autonomous: complete, independent and clear at a glance, explains (Soest, 2021). The user stories must also include acceptance criteria. Ultimately, the intention is for stakeholders to review and approve these user stories before they are considered documented requirements and wishes.


It is important to note that the user stories drawn up do not contain all requirements. They mainly include the Functional Requirements. In addition to these functional aspects, there are also the Non-Functional Requirements, such as the speed of the system response, the security levels or the user-friendliness (ScaledAgileFramework, 2023). In addition, this also includes Design Constraints, such as the choice of programming languages, technologies or hardware components. Both the Functional and Non-Functional requirements together form the scope of the project. However, certain items may not be explicitly stated, making it unclear whether they fall within the scope of the project. These items should be discussed and documented separately to provide complete clarity to both stakeholders and developers about what is and is not within the scope of the project.

4.4.3 Research plan
Various knowledge questions constantly arise during the development of the product. Many technologies or methods are often unknown to the developer and therefore require further research in advance. Moreover, the project starts with the research phase, but how exactly is research conducted and are the results useful and valid? In short, how is data collected and how do we analyze this data to ensure its usability and reliability.

Literature searches and prototyping comprised the majority of the research methods in the research phase, but a Multi-Criteria Analysis (hereinafter referred to as MCA) was also discussed. At the beginning of the research phase, the problem and goal are broadly determined. The next step was to investigate the best-suited technologies and resources for this problem. It always starts with a literature search; looking up possible solutions to the problem or analyzing existing projects with similar topics. It is always best practice to divide a large complex problem into smaller sub-problems. This way it is easier to find a suitable solution for the main problem.

Azure Environment
Perhaps the most interesting but complex component of this system lies in the Microsoft Azure landscape. The previous section explains which components the system consists of and how they work together. In this section we will take a closer look at the Azure environment that is used to recognize the products from the captures.

It starts with the Azure Blobstorage. A blob store is simply a cloud storage for unstructured data. A “blob” stands for “Binary Large Object” for short, which in our case is almost always an image. This blob storage stores all images necessary to recognize the products. This includes images such as the captures themselves, but also all images of the products themselves.
 	
 An Azure Dataset is created from all images of all possible products. We need this dataset after the next step to create an Azure Custom Model. In the Azure Machine Learning Data Labeling Service, we create so-called “labels”. These are purely the names we want to give to the products. Consider, for example: Coca Cola, Fanta, Venz Hagelslag, etc.

 	Now that the labels and dataset have been created, the training process can be started. In the dataset we can indicate where the object is located on each image and mark it. We can then give the object the corresponding label as shown in the image below. This process must be repeated on each image in the dataset. Subsequently, each labeled image must also be reviewed. To ensure that these are all correct.

 	Finally in the training process, we can export the Labeling project to a COCO file. This file is used to create the Custom Model. We can then start the real training, now Azure will train the custom model on our data using machine learning.
 	This training can be completely tailored to your own wishes. The time and method of training is easy to set. In addition, over-training is not possible; Azure automatically stops when the system thinks the model is no longer trainable.

Once the Custom Model has finished training, it is possible to use the model in Computer Vision. The vision is then trained on the objects we have labeled. Now the software is able to recognize these objects from other images, such as our captures from stock.

Your discussion on the Azure Environment and subsequent sections indicates a mix of technical detail and project workflow description, which is necessary for understanding the infrastructure and steps involved in product recognition. Below, I offer feedback on the Azure Environment section, along with general guidance for the remaining sections:

### Azure Environment
- Your explanation of the Azure Blob storage and its role in storing unstructured data is well-articulated.
- Clarification of terms like "Azure Dataset," "Data Labeling Service," and "COCO file" is useful for readers not familiar with Azure or machine learning.
- It may be beneficial to illustrate the process visually, perhaps with a flowchart, especially where you describe labeling and training data.
- You've communicated the ease of setting up the training process and the safeguards against overtraining, which are helpful details for technical stakeholders.
- Elaborate on how the trained model is validated for accuracy and the criteria for determining when Azure's automated stoppage is in line with project standards.

### 7.4 From Capture to Data
- This section should detail the workflow of capturing images from inventory, processing them, and extracting data. Discuss any challenges or considerations unique to your inventory environment, such as lighting or positioning variations.

### 7.5 Deployment
- Here, you should describe the steps to move from a trained model to an operational state in a production environment. Talk about deployment strategies and any automation or monitoring systems in place to support the deployment process.

### 8.0 Testing
#### 8.1 Intro
- Introduce the types of testing to be implemented (unit, integration, system, user acceptance) and link each to the corresponding phase in the project.

#### 8.2 Test strategy
- Your test strategy should cover the overall approach to ensuring that the system meets the defined requirements and functions correctly. Specify who will be involved, the tools to be used, and the scope and objectives of the tests.

#### 8.3 Reporting
- Detail the reporting mechanisms, frequency, and formats of test reports. Explain how these reports will support decision-making and project progress assessment.

#### 8.4 Metrics
- Define the key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics used to evaluate the testing process. Describe how these metrics contribute to quality assurance and the overall success of the project.

### 9.0 Results
- This section will present the outcomes of implementation and testing phases. Include both quantitative data (performance metrics, test results) and qualitative feedback (user satisfaction, stakeholder comments).

### 10. Conclusion
#### 10.1 Evaluation
- Reflect on the project's achievements in relation to the initial objectives and any learning opportunities that arose. Discuss any areas for improvement or recommendations for future enhancements.

- User-centered discussions and risk management strategies from earlier sections should be referenced as guiding principles throughout.
- Cross-reference related sections where appropriate to guide readers through the document comprehensively.
- Visual aids like charts, models, and diagrams can significantly enhance the understanding of complex workflows and systems.
